turns.content: I'm so frustrated, I just spent hours trying to understand why my for loop keeps breaking when I try to split a huge vector into smaller pieces instead of processing everything at once. Why does the standard iteration approach even feel wrong here?

That is actually a really common hurdle! You're hitting it because Rust's iterators are lazy, meaning they don't build a new list in memory until you ask them to. For your batching problem, the crate itertools has a helper called chunks that takes an iterator and a size parameter to yield fixed-size batches directly. It handles the remainder items automatically by creating extra empty batches or padding depending on your needs.
Oh okay so I don't need to write my own loop logic? Can you show me exactly how to chain chunks with a closure that does something for each batch?

Sure, you can just call the iterator method on your vector like this: vec_data.chunks(5).into_iter().for_each(|batch| { /* process items in this batch */ }). It returns an Iterator trait implementation that yields each chunk as a slice. If you need to group by another field instead of fixed size, itertools also has chunk_by which takes a key function to determine where one group ends and the next begins.
